title: HTTP Header Response Misconfiguration
description: HTTP security response headers help browsers enforce client-side protections such as HTTPS enforcement, clickjacking prevention, MIME sniffing protection, referrer leakage reduction, and content loading restrictions. Missing or weakly configured headers can reduce browser-side hardening and increase the impact of other vulnerabilities, such as cross-site scripting or clickjacking.
impact: Missing or weak browser security headers reduce client-side hardening and can increase the impact of other web vulnerabilities.
likelihood: Low for well-hardened applications and higher where defaults or inconsistent edge policies are in use.
recommendations: Configure appropriate HTTP security response headers across all affected applications and ensure they are applied consistently across frontend, API, admin, and supporting application hosts.
At minimum, configure HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options or frame-ancestors, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy where applicable.
Review exposed Server and X-Powered-By headers and remove unnecessary implementation detail disclosure where practical.
